title: Pleasant Events List
source_marker: Emotion Regulation Handout 16 (Emotion Regulation Worksheets 9, 10, 13) (p. 1 of 3)

Emotion Regulation Handout 16 (Emotion Regulation Worksheets 9, 10, 13) (p. 1 of 3)
Pleasant Events List 1.	пҒұ	Working on my car 2.	пҒұ	Planning a career 3.	пҒұ	Getting out of (paying down) debt 4.	пҒұ	Collecting things (baseball cards, coins,
stamps, rocks, shells, etc.) 5.	пҒұ	Going on vacation 6.	пҒұ	Thinking how it will be when I finish
school 7.	пҒұ	Recycling old items 8.	пҒұ	Going on a date 9.	пҒұ	Relaxing 10.	пҒұ	Going to or watching a movie 11.	пҒұ	Jogging, walking 12.	пҒұ	Thinking, вҖңI have done a full dayвҖҷs workвҖқ 13.	пҒұ	Listening to music 14.	пҒұ	Thinking about past parties 15.	пҒұ	Buying household gadgets 16.	пҒұ	Lying in the sun 17.	пҒұ	Planning a career change 18.	пҒұ	Laughing 19.	пҒұ	Thinking about past trips 20.	пҒұ	Listening to other people 21.	пҒұ	Reading magazines or newspapers 22.	пҒұ	Engaging in hobbies (stamp collecting,
model building, etc.) 23.	пҒұ	Spending an evening with good friends 24.	пҒұ	Planning a dayвҖҷs activities 25.	пҒұ	Meeting new people 26.	пҒұ	Remembering beautiful scenery 27.	пҒұ	Saving money 28.	пҒұ	Going home from work 29.	пҒұ	Eating 30.	пҒұ	Practicing karate, judo, yoga 31.	пҒұ	Thinking about retirement 32.	пҒұ	Repairing things around the house 33.	пҒұ	Working on machinery (cars, boats,
etc.) 34.	пҒұ	Remembering the words and deeds of
loving people 35.	пҒұ	Wearing shocking clothes 36.	пҒұ	Having quiet evenings 37.	пҒұ	Taking care of my plants 38.	пҒұ	Buying, selling stock 39.	пҒұ	Going swimming 40.	пҒұ	Doodling 41.	пҒұ	Exercising 42.	пҒұ	Collecting old things 43.	пҒұ	Going to a party 44.	пҒұ	Thinking about buying things 45.	пҒұ	Playing golf 46.	пҒұ	Playing soccer 47.	пҒұ	Flying kites 48.	пҒұ	Having discussions with friends 49.	пҒұ	Having family get-togethers 50.	пҒұ	Riding a bike or motorbike 51.	пҒұ	Running track 52.	пҒұ	Going camping 53.	пҒұ	Singing around the house 54.	пҒұ	Arranging flowers 55.	пҒұ	Practicing religion (going to church,
group praying, etc.) 56.	пҒұ	Organizing tools 57.	пҒұ	Going to the beach 58.	пҒұ	Thinking, вҖңIвҖҷm an OK personвҖқ 59.	пҒұ	Having a day with nothing to do 60.	пҒұ	Going to class reunions 61.	пҒұ	Going skating, skateboarding,
rollerblading 62.	пҒұ	Going sailing or motorboating 63.	пҒұ	Traveling or going on vacations 64.	пҒұ	Painting 65.	пҒұ	Doing something spontaneously 66.	пҒұ	Doing needlepoint, crewel, etc. 67.	пҒұ	Sleeping 68.	пҒұ	Driving 69.	пҒұ	Entertaining, giving parties 70.	пҒұ	Going to clubs (garden clubs, Parents
without Partners, etc.) 71.	пҒұ	Thinking about getting married 72.	пҒұ	Going hunting
(continued on next page)
Note. For adults or adolescents. Adapted from Linehan, M. M., Sharp, E., & Ivanoff, A. M. (1980, November). The Adult Pleasant Events Sched-
ule. Paper presented at the meeting of the Association for Advancement of Behavior Therapy, New York. Adapted by permission of the authors.

copyright_notice: Extracted from user-provided PDF: DBT Skills Training Handouts and Worksheets, Second Edition by Marsha M. Linehan. Respect source copyright and purchaser-use limits.

ourspace_kindness_pass.title: Pleasant Events List

main_doability_needs: bed-level or seated options by default
oxygen/breathing, pain, vertigo, fatigue, mobility, and recovery time respected
ADHD/autism supports: transitions, low-stimulation starts, tiny visible steps
partial completion and stopping before a crash count as success
no shame, no body blame, no pressure to push through pain or dysphoria
ADHD transition support before task demands
caregiver burnout recovery treated as care, not avoidance
protected rest, boundaries, decompression, sensory needs, and people-pleasing checks
no assumption that Jasper has unlimited caregiver capacity

household_rules: no hour block may hold more than two tasks/tools
skills may be used as one-breath or one-field versions
survival days load critical care first and skip optional worksheets
rest, boundaries, decompression, and asking for help count as care
skills are for support, not grades

universal_kindness_rules: This is a support tool, not a test.
One line, one checkbox, one breath, or one tiny step counts.
Skipping a skill because the body is unsafe, exhausted, dysregulated, or overloaded is allowed.
Do not stack this with more than one other task inside the same hour.
Survival-day version always wins over perfection.
william_accessibility_adaptations: Use bed-level, seated, voice-note, or caregiver-assisted versions when needed.
Avoid pain escalation, respiratory strain, unsafe transfers, and dysphoria-triggering language.
Break worksheets into one-field chunks; stopping before a crash is success.
jasper_accessibility_adaptations: Start with transition support before expecting focus.
Protect caregiver burnout recovery; rest and boundaries are valid completion.
Use body-doubling, timers, low-stimulation versions, and one-minute starts.
tiny_steps: Choose one sentence or tool that fits today.
Use the low-spoon version; no full worksheet is required.

routine_integration.start_the_day_use: eligible as a rotating daily skill, never more than one DBT/ADHD skill in an hour block
routine_integration.recommended_block: after body check or before decompression
